title: Odio — Collaborative Jam Session Recorder
problem: Musicians lose the best moments from their jam sessions. When they do record, the raw file is long, unedited, buried — nobody goes back and cleans it up. No tool lets a small band record on any device, pass the recording around in the car on the way home, each person trimming a song into its final form, and arrive with a clean versioned archive — all from a browser, no install, no DAW.
analysis: Pure web PWA (Next.js). No native app. Browser MediaRecorder API covers recording on iOS Safari 14.5+, Android Chrome, and desktop. The core primitive is versioning: every clip starts as v1 (raw recording), band members create new versions by marking cut regions, and any version can be frozen — triggering a server-side FFmpeg render. Versions are metadata only (cutMarks + annotations JSON) pointing at the same source audio. No simultaneous editing; linear versioning. Storage split: audio files (.aac) in Google Drive (recorder's account, free), all structured metadata (sessions, clips, versions, votes, comments, stamps) in a free-tier Postgres (Neon or Vercel Postgres). No Supabase. Auth: NextAuth.js + Google OAuth (drive.file scope). Real-time: polling every 5-10s. Edit state in localStorage as safety net; submit requires connection. External services: Google + free Postgres. Cost: $0/month.
successCriteria:
- Recorder opens browser (any device), records a session, all clips uploaded to Google Drive and metadata saved to Postgres
- Band member opens a link in mobile browser, opens a clip in the editor within 2 taps
- Editor loads waveform via Drive audio proxy, marks cuts, previews result without re-encoding
- Submitting a version writes to Postgres; others see it within 5-10s via polling
- Three people can each edit a different clip simultaneously, each producing their own version
- Freeze triggers server-side render, produces downloadable file within 10s for a typical 2-8 min clip
- Version history shows full chain with author, timestamp, description, and result duration
- Total infrastructure cost: $0/month
implementation:
**Stack:** Next.js (App Router) + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS, deployed on Vercel
**Recording:** Browser MediaRecorder API + Web Audio API (level meter) + Wake Lock API (screen-on)
**Waveform editing:** wavesurfer.js 7.x + Regions plugin
**Storage — audio:** Google Drive (resumable upload bypassing Vercel 4.5MB body limit; transcoded to AAC on finalize)
**Storage — metadata:** Neon Postgres free tier (sessions, clips, versions, votes, comments, stamps, bands, members)
**Auth:** NextAuth.js + Google OAuth (drive.file scope) + NextAuth DB adapter (Prisma + Neon, required to store recorder's refresh token for audio proxy)
**Audio proxy:** Next.js API route fetches audio from recorder's Drive using stored token, streams to client with Range support
**Real-time:** Client-side polling every 5-10s when session active
**Render:** fluent-ffmpeg + @ffmpeg-installer/ffmpeg in Next.js API route (triggered on freeze only)
**Edit safety:** localStorage (cut marks saved on every change, restored on editor reopen, cleared on submit)
**External services: 2** — Google (OAuth + Drive) + Neon (Postgres). Cost: $0/month.

decisions.rationale: Browser MediaRecorder API works on iOS Safari 14.5+, Android Chrome, and desktop. No install removes friction for bandmates who only need to edit/review. The 'in the car editing' use case requires web access — native would require app install on every bandmate's phone.
decisions.recommendation: Next.js PWA on Vercel. Add-to-home-screen on mobile for app-like feel.

decisions.decision: Google Drive for audio, Neon Postgres for metadata — no Supabase
decisions.rationale: Supabase Storage costs accumulate at ~100MB/session. Google Drive is free (15GB) for audio. Structured metadata (sessions, clips, versions, votes, comments) belongs in a relational DB for querying, not in JSON sidecars with ETag concurrency management. Neon Postgres free tier (512MB) handles years of band metadata. Both services are $0.
decisions.recommendation: Audio files (.aac): Google Drive, recorder's account. Metadata: Neon Postgres via Prisma ORM. Auth tokens: NextAuth DB adapter writing to same Neon instance.

decisions.decision: NextAuth DB adapter required — not JWT-only
decisions.rationale: The audio proxy route serves the recorder's Drive files to all band members. To do this, the server needs the recorder's valid refresh token when any member makes a request — not just when the recorder is logged in. NextAuth's DB adapter stores access_token and refresh_token in Postgres per user, enabling server-side token lookup by user email. JWT-only strategy cannot support this cross-user token access.
decisions.recommendation: NextAuth with Prisma adapter. Tokens stored in Postgres accounts table. Proxy route looks up recorder's token by bandId → band.createdBy → accounts.userId.

decisions.decision: Versioning as core primitive — versions are metadata, not audio files
decisions.rationale: Storing a full audio file per version would be prohibitively expensive. Versions (cutMarks + annotations JSON) are bytes vs megabytes. Source audio stored once; versions are lightweight edit instructions applied at render time or virtually during preview.
decisions.recommendation: versions array inside .meta.json: [{cutMarks, annotations, description, createdBy, fromVersionId}]. Source audio never mutated.

decisions.decision: Linear versioning only — no simultaneous co-editing
decisions.rationale: Real-time simultaneous editing (CRDT/Y.js) adds significant complexity. The real use case is 3 people each editing a different song. Linear versioning covers all realistic scenarios without conflict resolution logic.
decisions.recommendation: Append-only version chain. 'Edit from this version' creates a new version. No locking, no presence cursors.

decisions.decision: localStorage for in-progress edit safety — no offline sync
decisions.rationale: Full offline support adds complexity. The real need is simpler: if the browser crashes mid-edit, cut marks shouldn't be lost. localStorage solves this with minimal code. Submit still requires a connection — that's acceptable.
decisions.recommendation: Save cut marks to localStorage on every change. Restore draft on editor reopen. Clear on successful submit.

decisions.decision: Resumable Drive upload from browser — bypass Vercel body limit
decisions.rationale: Vercel's 4.5MB request body limit cannot accommodate a 30-min audio recording (~43MB). Resumable uploads: server generates a Drive upload session URL, client uploads directly to Drive. Audio never passes through Vercel.
decisions.recommendation: POST /api/upload/init → resumable session URL → browser uploads to Drive → POST /api/upload/finalize → server transcodes to AAC + creates .meta.json.

knownLimitations: iOS Safari stops MediaRecorder if screen locks or user switches tabs — Wake Lock API (iOS 16.4+) mitigates; warning shown before recording
Concurrent JSON writes by two members at the same moment trigger a 412 conflict — rare at band scale, handled by reload-and-retry
Polling (not WebSocket) — new versions/votes appear within 5-10s, not instantly
MediaRecorder output format varies by browser (WebM/Opus on Chrome, AAC/MP4 on iOS) — transcoded to AAC on upload
Recorder's Google Drive storage used for band audio — 15GB free, sufficient for many sessions

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Platform pivot to web-only PWA. Versioning as core primitive. PRD v0.2 + Architecture v0.2 + Epics v0.2.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Removed Supabase entirely. Google Drive for audio + .meta.json sidecars. NextAuth.js. localStorage safety net only. Architecture v0.3.
